Now let me systematically explore the ActiveRecord save flow. I'll start with the key modules involved in persistence.

Let me map the main persistence-related directories in ActiveRecord:
1. `activerecord/lib/active_record/persistence.rb` - the main save entry point
2. `activerecord/lib/active_record/callbacks.rb` - save callbacks
3. `activerecord/lib/active_record/validations.rb` - validation integration
4. `activerecord/lib/active_record/transactions.rb` - transaction handling
5. `activerecord/lib/active_record/associations.rb` - association cascading
I'll examine these files together to understand how they interact during a save operation.

      attribute_names = attributes_for_create(attribute_names)

      self.class.with_connection do |connection|
        returning_columns = self.class._returning_columns_for_insert(connection)

        returning_values = self.class._insert_record(
          connection,
          attributes_with_values(attribute_names),
          returning_columns
        )

          _write_attribute(column, value) if !_read_attribute(column)

      @previously_new_record = true

      yield(self) if block_given?

      id
    end"}

{"id":"ruby:activerecord/lib/active_record/persistence.rb#_insert_record@238","source":"def _insert_record(connection, values, returning) # :nodoc:
        primary_key = self.primary_key
        primary_key_value = nil

        if prefetch_primary_key? && primary_key
          values[primary_key] ||= begin
            primary_key_value = next_sequence_value
            _default_attributes[primary_key].with_cast_value(primary_key_value)
          end
        end

        im = Arel::InsertManager.new(arel_table)

        with_connection do |c|
          if values.empty?
            im.insert(connection.empty_insert_statement_value(primary_key))
          else
            im.insert(values.transform_keys { |name| arel_table[name] })
          end

          connection.insert(
            im, "#{self} Create", primary_key || false, primary_key_value,
            returning: returning
          )
        end
      end"}

{"id":"ruby:activerecord/lib/active_record/persistence.rb#_update_record@265","other_candidates":["ruby:activerecord/lib/active_record/persistence.rb#_update_record@902"],"source":"def _update_record(values, constraints) # :nodoc:
        constraints = constraints.map { |name, value| predicate_builder[name, value] }

        default_constraint = build_default_constraint
        constraints << default_constraint if default_constraint

        if current_scope = self.global_current_scope
          constraints << current_scope.where_clause.ast
        end

        um = Arel::UpdateManager.new(arel_table)
        um.set(values.transform_keys { |name| arel_table[name] })
        um.wheres = constraints

        with_connection do |c|
          c.update(um, "#{self} Update")
        end
      end"}

{"id":"ruby:activerecord/lib/active_record/autosave_association.rb#add_autosave_association_callbacks@189","source":"def add_autosave_association_callbacks(reflection)
          save_method = :"autosave_associated_records_for_#{reflection.name}"

          if reflection.collection?
            around_save :around_save_collection_association

            define_non_cyclic_method(save_method) { save_collection_association(reflection) }
            # Doesn't use after_save as that would save associations added in after_create/after_update twice
            after_create save_method
            after_update save_method
          elsif reflection.has_one?
            define_non_cyclic_method(save_method) { save_has_one_association(reflection) }
            # Configures two callbacks instead of a single after_save so that
            # the model may rely on their execution order relative to its
            # own callbacks.
            #
            # For example, given that after_creates run before after_saves, if
            # we configured instead an after_save there would be no way to fire
            # a custom after_create callback after the child association gets
            # created.
            after_create save_method
            after_update save_method
          else
            define_non_cyclic_method(save_method) { throw(:abort) if save_belongs_to_association(reflection) == false }
            before_save save_method
          end

          define_autosave_validation_callbacks(reflection)
        end"}

{"id":"ruby:activerecord/lib/active_record/autosave_association.rb#save_belongs_to_association@492","source":"def save_belongs_to_association(reflection)
        association = association_instance_get(reflection.name)
        return unless association && association.loaded? && !association.stale_target?

        record = association.load_target
        if record && !record.destroyed?
          autosave = reflection.options[:autosave]

          if autosave && record.marked_for_destruction?
            foreign_key = Array(reflection.foreign_key)
            foreign_key.each { |key| self[key] = nil }
            record.destroy
          elsif autosave != false
            saved = record.save(validate: !autosave) if record.new_record? || (autosave && record.changed_for_autosave?)

            if association.updated?
              primary_key = Array(compute_primary_key(reflection, record)).map(&:to_s)
              foreign_key = Array(reflection.foreign_key)

              primary_key_foreign_key_pairs = primary_key.zip(foreign_key)
              primary_key_foreign_key_pairs.each do |primary_key, foreign_key|
                association_id = record._read_attribute(primary_key)
                self[foreign_key] = association_id unless self[foreign_key] == association_id
              end
              association.loaded!
            end

            saved if autosave
          end
        end
      end"}

        association = association_instance_get(reflection.name)

{"id":"ruby:activerecord/lib/active_record/autosave_association.rb#save_collection_association@375","source":"def save_collection_association(reflection)
        if association = association_instance_get(reflection.name)
          autosave = reflection.options[:autosave]

          # By saving the instance variable in a local variable,
          # we make the whole callback re-entrant.
          new_record_before_save = @new_record_before_save

          # reconstruct the scope now that we know the owner's id
          association.reset_scope

          if records = associated_records_to_validate_or_save(association, new_record_before_save, autosave)
            if autosave
              records_to_destroy = records.select(&:marked_for_destruction?)
              records_to_destroy.each { |record| association.destroy(record) }
              records -= records_to_destroy
            end

            records.each do |record|
              next if record.destroyed?

              saved = true

              if autosave != false && (new_record_before_save || record.new_record?)
                association.set_inverse_instance(record)

                if autosave
                  saved = association.insert_record(record, false)
                elsif !reflection.nested?
                  association_saved = association.insert_record(record)

                  if reflection.validate?
                    errors.add(reflection.name) unless association_saved
                    saved = association_saved
                  end
                end
              elsif autosave
                saved = record.save(validate: false)
              end

              raise(RecordInvalid.new(association.owner)) unless saved
            end
          end
        end
      end"}

## The complete save flow

### 1. Entry point: `Validations#save` (validations.rb:47)

The `save` method is defined in multiple modules, and these modules are prepended/included in a specific order. The method resolution order (MRO) means the call traverses through multiple `super` calls.

When you call `record.save`, the first definition hit is in `Validations`:
- `perform_validations(options)` gets called, which either skips validation if `options[:validate] == false` or runs `valid?` to trigger `ActiveModel::Validations` callbacks in the `:create` or `:update` context
- Autosave association validation callbacks also execute here through `define_autosave_validation_callbacks`
- If validation fails, the method returns `false` (or raises `RecordInvalid` for `save!`), otherwise it calls `super` to continue the chain

### 2. Transaction wrapping: `Transactions#save` (transactions.rb:361)

The next module in the MRO is `Transactions`

—it wraps the save in a transaction by calling `with_transaction_returning_status { super }`, which gets a database connection, checks if one's already open, opens a new transaction block if needed, registers the record with the transaction to receive callbacks later, snapshots the current record state for potential rollback, yields to continue the chain, and raises `ActiveRecord::Rollback` if the result is falsy to abort the transaction.

### 3. Callback wr

### 4. Dispatch: `Persistence#create_or_update` checks if the record is readonly or destroyed, then branches to either `_create_record` for inserts or `_update_record` for updates.

### 5a. INSERT path: `Callbacks#_create_record` wraps the creation in `_run_create_callbacks { super }` to fire before and after create callbacks.

Inside `Persistence#_create_record`, I'm filtering attributes through `attributes_for_create` to exclude virtual columns and remove the primary key if it's nil so the database can auto-generate it. Then I'm casting all included attribute values and calling the class method `_insert_record`, which handles sequence prefetching for Postgres, builds an Arel insert manager, executes the SQL INSERT via the connection, and returns any database-generated values like the auto-incremented id or timestamp columns.

After the insert completes, I'm writing those returned values back to the record using `_write_attribute`, setting `@new_record` to false and `@previously_new_record` to true, then firing the `after_create` and `after_save` callbacks as the callback block unwinds.

For the UPDATE path, `Callbacks#_update_record` wraps the update in callbacks that also handle timestamp recording, then delegates to `Persistence#_update_record`, which filters attributes through `attributes_for_update` to exclude readonly columns and counter cache attributes.

If there are no attributes left to update, it sets `affected_rows = 0` but still marks the update callback to fire; otherwise it builds the constraint predicates from the primary key or query constraints, applies any global scope, constructs an `Arel::UpdateManager`, and executes the SQL UPDATE via the connection, using the affected rows count to determine whether to trigger the update callback.

If the transaction rolls back instead, `rolledback!` is called to fire `after_rollback` callbacks and restore each record to its pre-transaction state.

Each level wraps the next via `super`. Reading the code linearly you see each one delegate downward.

### Stage 1 — Validation gate (`validations.rb:47`)

```ruby
def save(**options)
  perform_validations(options) ? super : false
end
```

### Stage 2 — Transaction envelope (`transactions.rb:361`, `409`)

```ruby

```

1. Acquires a connection and checks whether a transaction is **already open** (`ensure_finalize = !connection.transaction_open?`).
2. Opens `connection.transaction { ... }` — if one is already open this becomes a savepoint, not a new outer transaction.
3. Calls `add_to_transaction` → `connection.add_transaction_record(self, ensure_finalize)`. This registers the record with the transaction manager so it receives `committed!` or `rolledback!` when the outermost transaction settles.
4. Calls `remember_transaction_record_state` — snapshots `@new_record`, `@previously_new_record`, `@destroyed`, `@attributes`, and the current `id` into `@_start_transaction_state`. This is the rollback restore point.
5. Yields (executes all remaining stages inside the DB transaction).
6. If the result is falsy, raises `ActiveRecord::Rollback` to abort — so a `save` returning `false` rolls back the transaction automatically.

---

### Stage 3 — Save callbacks (`callbacks.rb:440`)

```ruby

```

`_run_save_callbacks` fires `before_save` hooks, yields to the next stage, then fires `after_save` hooks on the way back out. The `belongs_to` autosave callback is registered as a `before_save`, so it runs here (more on that below).

---

### Stage 4 — New-vs-existing dispatch (`persistence.rb:893`)

```ruby
def create_or_update(**, &block)
  _raise_readonly_record_error if readonly?
  return false if destroyed?
  result = new_record? ? _create_record(&block) : _update_record(&block)
  result != false

```

### Stage 5a — INSERT path

**`Callbacks#_create_record` (`callbacks.rb:444`)**:

```ruby

```

**`Persistence#_create_record` (`persistence.rb:922`)**:

1. **Attribute filtering** via `attributes_for_create` (`attribute_methods.rb:514`):

2. **SQL** via `self.class._insert_record(connection, values, returning_columns)` (`persistence.rb:238`):
   - Builds an `Arel::InsertManager` with the filtered, cast values.
   - Calls `connection.insert(im, ...)`, which materialises the SQL and sends it to the DB.
   - Returns any `RETURNING` columns (DB-generated id, `created_at`, etc.).

3. **Write-back** — iterates `returning_columns.zip(returning_values)` and calls `_write_attribute` for any column that wasn't already set (e.g., the auto-generated id lands back on the model).

4. Sets `@new_record = false` and `@previously_new_record = true`.

---

### Stage 5b — UPDATE path

**`Persistence#_update_record` (`persistence.rb:902`)**:

1. **Attribute filtering** via `attributes_for_update` (`attribute_methods.rb:503`):

### Stage 6 — Association cascading (`autosave_association.rb`)

Autosave callbacks are registered per-association by `add_autosave_association_callbacks` (`autosave_association.rb:189`). The timing is different for each association direction:

**`belongs_to` → `before_save`**

`save_belongs_to_association` runs before the owner record is written to the DB. If the parent is new or dirty (and `autosave: true`), it saves the parent first. After saving, it copies the parent's PK into the owner's foreign-key column — that value then gets included in the owner's INSERT/UPDATE issued in Stage 5.

**`has_one` → `after_create` + `after_update`**

`save_has_one_association` runs after the owner is persisted (and therefore has an id). It sets the child's FK to the owner's PK and calls `record.save`. If `autosave: true` and the child's save returns false, it raises `ActiveRecord::Rollback` to abort the enclosing transaction.

- Resets the association scope now that the owner has an id.
- Destroys records marked for destruction (if `autosave: true`).
- For each surviving record: calls `association.insert_record` (new records) or `record.save(validate: false)` (dirty records when `autosave: true`).
- Raises `RecordInvalid` on failure, which propagates into the DB transaction and triggers a rollback.

All of this happens inside the `connection.transaction` block opened in Stage 2, so any failure anywhere in the association cascade rolls back every write.

---

### Stage 7 — Transaction outcome

When the outermost `connection.transaction` block exits, the connection's transaction manager notifies every registered record:

**On commit → `committed!` (`transactions.rb:381`)**:
```ruby
def committed!(should_run_callbacks: true)
  @_start_transaction_state = nil       # discard the snapshot
  _run_commit_callbacks                 # fires after_commit / after_save_commit etc.
ensure
  @_committed_already_called = @_trigger_update_callback = @_trigger_destroy_callback = false
end
```
`after_commit` (and its shorthand aliases `after_create_commit`, `after_update_commit`) runs here, outside the DB transaction. The `@_trigger_update_callback` flag set in Stage 5b controls which variant fires.

**On rollback → `rolledback!` (`transactions.rb:393`)**:
```ruby
def rolledback!(force_restore_state: false, should_run_callbacks: true)
  _run_rollback_callbacks
ensure
  restore_transaction_record_state(force_restore_state)
  clear_transaction_record_state
end
```
`restore_transaction_record_state` (`transactions.rb:468`) replays the snapshot captured at Stage 2 — it reverts `@new_record`, `@destroyed`, `@attributes` (including the id), and clears `@mutations_from_database` and `@mutations_before_last_save`. The in-memory object goes back to looking exactly as it did before `save` was called.

---

### How the stages connect — the dependency chain

```
caller calls save
  │
  ├─ [Validations] run validators (including autosave validates_associated)
  │    ↓ if valid
  ├─ [Transactions] open/join a DB transaction; register record; snapshot state
  │    ↓
  ├─ [Callbacks] fire before_save
  │    │  ← belongs_to autosave fires here (saves parent, writes FK into owner)
  │    ↓
  ├─ [Persistence] choose INSERT vs UPDATE
  │    ↓
  ├─ [Callbacks] fire before_create / before_update
  │    ↓
  ├─ [Persistence] filter attributes → build Arel → connection.insert / .update
  │    ↓
  ├─ [Callbacks] fire after_create / after_update
  │    │  ← has_one / has_many autosave fires here (saves children with FK set to owner PK)
  │    ↓
  ├─ [Callbacks] fire after_save
  │    ↓
  └─ [Transactions] transaction commits or rolls back

       └─ rollback → rolledback! → after_rollback + in-memory state restored
```

The key coupling points for your planned change:
